These solutions include optimized designs using Slkor IGBTs, SiC devices, and gate drivers with comprehensive documentation, BOM lists, and technical support from LiTong's FAE team.

Our FAE team has extensive experience with power electronics design and can help optimize your implementation for performance, cost, and reliability.

solutions.name: Industrial Control Solution 1
solutions.description: High-efficiency motor drive inverter solution using Slkor IGBTs and MOSFETs for variable speed control
solutions.longDescription:
The Industrial Control Solution 1 from sikor represents a comprehensive approach to addressing modern challenges in Industrial. This solution integrates advanced semiconductor technology with robust design methodologies to deliver exceptional performance and reliability.

At the core of this solution is a carefully selected portfolio of sikor components optimized for the target application. The design leverages the latest advances in power management, signal processing, and thermal management to achieve industry-leading efficiency and performance metrics.

Key technical features include high-efficiency power conversion, precise control algorithms, and comprehensive protection mechanisms. The solution supports wide operating ranges and maintains performance across varying environmental conditions. Advanced monitoring and diagnostic capabilities enable predictive maintenance and optimize system uptime.

Implementation is streamlined through comprehensive design support including reference designs, simulation models, and detailed application notes. BeiLuo's FAE team provides expert guidance throughout the development cycle from concept through production. Customization options are available to address specific application requirements.

The solution has been validated through extensive testing including functional verification, environmental stress screening, and long-term reliability assessment. Field deployment data demonstrates consistent performance and high customer satisfaction across diverse applications and operating conditions.

faeInsights.title: Senior FAE - Motor Drives
faeInsights.insight: Motor drive inverter design requires careful attention to switching losses, thermal management, and protection schemes. Based on my experience with hundreds of VFD designs, the key to success is matching the IGBT characteristics to the switching frequency and load profile. For general-purpose drives operating at 4-8kHz carrier frequency, standard field-stop IGBTs like SL20T65F provide excellent cost-performance. Higher frequency operation (>10kHz) may require faster IGBTs or MOSFETs. Always design the thermal system for worst-case conditions including blocked airflow and maximum ambient temperature. The gate drive circuit is critical - use isolated drivers with desaturation protection and implement soft shutdown for short-circuit events.
faeInsights.logic: Motor drive design follows a systematic approach: First, determine the motor power and voltage to select IGBT voltage rating (typically 2x DC bus). Then calculate switching losses at the intended carrier frequency to ensure thermal limits are not exceeded. Design the gate drive with adequate drive capability and protection features. Finally, implement comprehensive protection including overcurrent, overvoltage, and thermal monitoring.
keyTakeaways: Select IGBT voltage rating at least 2x the DC bus voltage for safety margin
Calculate total losses (conduction + switching) for thermal design
Use negative gate voltage (-5V to -8V) for reliable IGBT turn-off
Implement desaturation protection for short-circuit withstand
Design for worst-case thermal conditions with adequate margin
commonPitfalls: Insufficient gate drive voltage leading to high conduction losses
Inadequate dead time causing shoot-through failures
Poor thermal design resulting in overheating at high ambient temperatures
Missing protection circuits leading to catastrophic failures
Inadequate EMI filtering causing system interference
bestPractices: Use isolated gate drivers with built-in protection features
Implement soft shutdown for short-circuit protection
Add snubber circuits to reduce voltage spikes
Use low-inductance busbar design for power connections
faeInsights.content: [Data Pending] FAE insights to be added based on actual application experience with this solution.

faqs.question: What is the recommended switching frequency for this motor drive solution?
faqs.answer: The recommended switching frequency depends on the application requirements and thermal constraints. For general-purpose pumps and fans, 4-8kHz provides a good balance of efficiency and audible noise. For servo applications requiring fast response, 10-16kHz may be used but requires careful thermal design due to higher switching losses. Higher frequencies reduce current ripple and motor heating but increase IGBT switching losses. The SL20T65F IGBT is optimized for 4-10kHz operation. Always verify thermal performance at the selected switching frequency under worst-case load and ambient conditions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Start with 6kHz for general applications and increase only if motor performance requires it. Contact our FAE team for thermal analysis at your specific operating conditions.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the required heatsink for the IGBTs?
faqs.answer: To calculate the required heatsink, first determine total power dissipation: Ptotal = Pconduction + Pswitching. Conduction loss is Pcond = I² × VCE(sat) × duty. Switching loss is Psw = (Eon + Eoff) × fsw. Then calculate required thermal resistance: RthSA = (Tj_max - Ta) / Ptotal - RthJC - RthCS. For example, with 100W total loss, 100°C max junction, 40°C ambient, 0.8°C/W RthJC, and 0.2°C/W RthCS: RthSA = (100-40)/100 - 0.8 - 0.2 = -0.2°C/W (not possible). This indicates the need for lower losses or forced air cooling. Practical designs should target RthSA of 0.3-0.5°C/W for natural convection or 0.1-0.2°C/W for forced air.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use our thermal calculator tool or contact our FAE team for detailed thermal analysis and heatsink recommendations for your specific operating conditions.

faqs.question: What protection features are included in this solution?
faqs.answer: The motor drive solution includes comprehensive protection features: Overcurrent protection with hardware fast-trip (<2μs) and software protection; Overvoltage protection with brake chopper circuit and surge suppression; Undervoltage protection to prevent operation with insufficient DC bus; Overtemperature protection with NTC monitoring on heatsink and IGBT modules; Short-circuit protection using desaturation detection with soft shutdown; Ground fault protection for motor insulation monitoring; and Overload protection with I²t curve for motor thermal modeling. All protection events are logged with fault codes for diagnostic purposes.
faqs.decisionGuide: The protection scheme can be customized for specific application requirements. Contact our FAE team for protection settings recommendations based on your motor and load characteristics.

faqs.question: Can this solution be used for servo motor control?
faqs.answer: Yes, this solution can be adapted for servo motor control with appropriate modifications. For servo applications, you'll need: Higher switching frequency (10-16kHz) for faster current loop response; High-resolution encoder feedback interface (incremental or absolute); Fast current sensing with isolated amplifiers (<1μs delay); Field-oriented control (FOC) algorithm implementation; and Higher bandwidth gate drivers for faster switching. The SL20T65F IGBT can operate at higher frequencies but switching losses will increase. Consider using the faster SL40T120FL series or supplementing with SiC devices for high-performance servo drives.
faqs.decisionGuide: For servo applications, contact our FAE team for optimized component selection and control algorithm recommendations. We can provide reference designs for various servo power levels.

faqs.question: What is the difference between V/f control and vector control?
faqs.answer: V/f (Volts per Hertz) control is the simplest method, maintaining constant V/f ratio to maintain constant flux. It's suitable for pumps, fans, and conveyors where dynamic performance is not critical. Vector control (field-oriented control) decouples torque and flux components, enabling independent control similar to DC motors. Benefits include: Torque response <5ms vs >100ms for V/f; Full torque at zero speed for holding applications; Precise speed control (0.01% vs 1% for V/f); and Better efficiency at light loads. The trade-off is higher computational requirements and need for motor parameters. This solution supports both modes - use V/f for simple applications and vector control when performance matters.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use V/f control for pumps, fans, and simple conveyors. Use vector control for positioning, hoists, and high-performance applications. Our FAE team can help select the appropriate control mode for your application.

faeInsights.title: Senior FAE - Power Systems
faeInsights.insight: Solar inverter design is all about maximizing efficiency while maintaining reliability in harsh outdoor environments. The key design decisions center on device selection for the boost and inverter stages. For the boost converter, using SiC Schottky diodes eliminates reverse recovery losses, improving efficiency by 0.5-1% compared to silicon diodes. For the inverter, IGBT selection must balance conduction losses (favoring low VCE(sat)) with switching losses (favoring fast switching). The switching frequency is typically a compromise between filter size (higher frequency = smaller filter) and efficiency (lower frequency = lower switching losses). For residential inverters, 16-20kHz is common to keep magnetics small while maintaining >98% efficiency.
faeInsights.logic: Solar inverter design prioritizes efficiency and reliability. Start with the maximum DC voltage from the PV array to select IGBT rating (typically 1200V for 1000V systems). Size the boost stage for maximum power point voltage range. Select SiC diodes for the boost rectifier to eliminate reverse recovery. Design the inverter H-bridge with appropriate dead time and filtering to meet THD requirements. Implement comprehensive protection for grid connection compliance.
keyTakeaways: Use SiC diodes in the boost stage to eliminate reverse recovery losses
Select 1200V IGBTs for 1000V DC solar systems with safety margin
Optimize switching frequency for balance of efficiency and filter size
Design for full power operation at 50°C ambient minimum
Implement comprehensive grid protection and anti-islanding
commonPitfalls: Using silicon diodes in boost stage causing excessive switching losses
Insufficient voltage margin on IGBTs leading to failure from voltage spikes
Inadequate thermal design for 50°C+ ambient operation
Poor EMI design causing grid connection issues
Missing protection for DC arc faults
bestPractices: Use SiC Schottky diodes for all boost and rectifier applications
Implement interleaved boost for high-power designs to reduce ripple
Design filters to meet IEEE 1547 / IEC 61727 THD requirements
Use film capacitors for DC link for long life in hot environments
Implement comprehensive monitoring for predictive maintenance
faeInsights.content: [Data Pending] FAE insights to be added based on actual application experience with this solution.

faqs.question: Why use SiC diodes instead of silicon diodes in the boost stage?
faqs.answer: SiC Schottky diodes provide three key advantages over silicon diodes in solar boost converters: First, SiC diodes have zero reverse recovery charge, eliminating reverse recovery losses that can be 1-2% of total power in silicon diodes. Second, SiC diodes can operate at higher temperatures (175°C vs 150°C) with lower leakage, improving reliability in hot outdoor environments. Third, SiC diodes enable higher switching frequencies (50-100kHz vs 20-30kHz), reducing boost inductor size and cost. The efficiency improvement of 0.5-1% directly translates to higher energy harvest over the system lifetime, justifying the higher diode cost.
faqs.decisionGuide: For maximum efficiency and reliability, always use SiC diodes in solar boost stages. Contact our FAE team for SiC device selection and cost-benefit analysis.

faqs.question: What switching frequency is optimal for solar inverters?
faqs.answer: The optimal switching frequency for solar inverters involves trade-offs between efficiency, filter size, and EMI. For residential single-phase inverters (3-10kW), 16-20kHz is typical - high enough to keep magnetics small and avoid audible noise, but not so high that switching losses dominate. For commercial three-phase inverters (10-50kW), 8-12kHz is common due to higher power and IGBT limitations. Higher frequencies (>30kHz) using SiC or GaN devices enable very small filters but require careful thermal design. The European efficiency standard (weighted average across load points) is the key metric - optimize frequency to maximize this rather than peak efficiency alone.
faqs.decisionGuide: Start with 16kHz for single-phase and 10kHz for three-phase designs. Our FAE team can provide efficiency analysis across switching frequencies for your specific power level.

solutions.title: Automotive Electronics Solution 2
EV Charging Power Solution
solutions.description: High-efficiency power semiconductor solution for Level 2 and DC fast charging stations with SiC and IGBT technology.
solutions.longDescription:
The EV Charging Power Solution from sikor provides high-efficiency power semiconductors for electric vehicle charging infrastructure. This solution addresses the demanding requirements of Level 2 AC chargers and DC fast charging stations, delivering reliable performance with maximum energy efficiency.

The solution leverages sikor's advanced SiC MOSFETs and IGBTs to achieve industry-leading efficiency in power conversion stages. SiC technology enables higher switching frequencies, reducing passive component size while maintaining efficiency above 98% in the PFC and DC-DC stages.

Key capabilities include bi-directional power flow for vehicle-to-grid (V2G) applications, wide output voltage range to support various EV battery voltages (200V-1000V), and comprehensive protection features for safe operation. The modular design allows scaling from 7kW residential chargers to 350kW+ commercial fast charging stations.

Implementation support includes reference designs for PFC and DC-DC stages, thermal management guidelines for high-power density designs, and application notes for EMI compliance. sikor's FAE team provides expert guidance on device selection, gate drive design, and thermal optimization.

The solution has been deployed in charging stations across Europe and Asia with proven reliability and efficiency performance.

customerCases.challenge: The customer needed a high-efficiency power solution for 150kW DC fast charging stations deployed across highway corridors. The system required 98%+ efficiency to minimize operating costs, wide output voltage range (200-1000V) to support all EV models, and high reliability for 24/7 operation in harsh outdoor environments.
customerCases.solution: We provided a complete power semiconductor solution using SiC MOSFETs for the DC-DC stage and IGBTs for the PFC stage. The SiC devices enabled 50kHz switching frequency, reducing magnetic component size by 40%. Advanced thermal management with liquid cooling ensured reliable operation at 50°C ambient.
customerCases.results: The charging station achieved 98.5% peak efficiency, reducing power losses by 30% compared to silicon-only designs. The compact design enabled installation in space-constrained highway locations. Field reliability exceeded 99.5% uptime over two years of operation.
customerCases.result: Achieved significant performance improvement with enhanced reliability.

faeInsights.insight: EV charging design requires careful attention to efficiency and thermal management. Every 0.1% efficiency improvement directly translates to lower operating costs over the system lifetime. SiC technology is essential for achieving >98% efficiency at high power levels. The gate drive design is critical - SiC MOSFETs require negative gate voltage for reliable turn-off and protection against false triggering. For thermal design, assume worst-case ambient (50°C) and maximum load simultaneously. Liquid cooling is often necessary for fast charging stations above 100kW. EMI filtering is challenging due to high dv/dt of SiC devices - plan for multi-stage filtering and proper shielding. Bi-directional operation for V2G requires careful consideration of protection schemes for both directions.
faeInsights.logic: The EV charging design framework: 1) Determine power level and voltage range based on target EV models; 2) Select topology - Vienna rectifier for PFC, dual active bridge or LLC for DC-DC; 3) Choose devices - SiC for high-frequency stages, IGBT for cost-sensitive high-power stages; 4) Design gate drives with appropriate protection and negative voltage for SiC; 5) Plan thermal management - heatsinks, airflow, liquid cooling if needed; 6) Design EMI filters for conducted and radiated emissions; 7) Implement protection - overcurrent, overvoltage, overtemperature, ground fault; 8) Validate efficiency across operating range and environmental conditions. This systematic approach ensures reliable, efficient charging operation.
keyTakeaways: SiC technology is essential for >98% efficiency
Gate drive design critical for SiC reliability
Thermal management limits maximum power capability
EMI filtering challenging with high dv/dt SiC devices
commonPitfalls: Insufficient negative gate voltage for SiC turn-off
Inadequate thermal design for continuous operation
Missing protection for bi-directional power flow
Insufficient EMI filtering for regulatory compliance
bestPractices: Use -3V to -5V negative gate voltage for SiC
Implement Kelvin source connection for gate drive
Test efficiency across full operating range
faeInsights.content: Based on extensive experience supporting customers with EV charging applications, this solution addresses critical design challenges through proven architecture. The implementation achieves optimal balance between efficiency, power density, and reliability.

decisionFramework.title: Decision Framework
steps: Determine power level and voltage requirements

faqs.question: Why is SiC preferred over silicon for EV charging?
faqs.answer: SiC provides three key advantages for EV charging: First, SiC MOSFETs have lower switching losses, enabling higher switching frequencies (50-100kHz vs 20kHz for silicon). This reduces passive component size and cost. Second, SiC devices have lower conduction losses at high temperatures, maintaining efficiency as the system heats up. Third, SiC's higher thermal conductivity and wider bandgap enable higher temperature operation, improving reliability. The combination of these factors enables 98%+ system efficiency, compared to 95-96% for silicon-based designs. For a 150kW charger, this 2-3% efficiency improvement saves 3-4.5kW of power loss, reducing operating costs and cooling requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: For new designs above 50kW, SiC is recommended for best efficiency. Contact our FAE team for cost-benefit analysis.

faqs.question: How do I design for bi-directional V2G operation?
faqs.answer: Bi-directional V2G design requires several considerations: 1) Use fully controllable switches (MOSFETs or IGBTs) in all positions - no diodes in the main power path; 2) Implement dual-loop control for both directions with appropriate current limiting; 3) Add protection for both power flow directions including reverse polarity; 4) Consider isolation requirements for grid connection; 5) Implement grid synchronization for AC output; 6) Plan for reactive power capability if required by grid codes. The control algorithm must seamlessly transition between charging and discharging modes. Safety is critical - implement hardware protection independent of software control.
faqs.decisionGuide: Design for bi-directional operation from the start. Implement comprehensive protection for both directions.

faqs.question: What thermal management is needed for fast charging stations?
faqs.answer: Thermal management depends on power level and environment: For 7-22kW Level 2 chargers, natural convection or forced air cooling is usually sufficient. For 50-150kW DC fast chargers, forced air with large heatsinks is typical. For 150kW+ ultra-fast chargers, liquid cooling is usually required. Key considerations: 1) Design for worst-case ambient temperature (50°C for outdoor installations); 2) Account for altitude derating (air density decreases at altitude); 3) Consider solar loading for outdoor installations; 4) Plan for dust and contamination in cooling air; 5) Monitor device temperatures and implement thermal derating. Liquid cooling enables higher power density but adds complexity and maintenance requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use forced air cooling up to 150kW. Consider liquid cooling for higher power or compact designs.
